edge: Language models can be augmented with a context retriever to incorporate knowledge from large external databases.
Leveraging retrieved context allows the neural network to not have to memorize the massive amount of world knowledge within its internal parameters.
Leveraging retrieved context leads to better parameter efficiency.
Leveraging retrieved context leads to better interpretability.
Leveraging retrieved context leads to better modularity.
The proposed architecture for incorporating external context into language models is based on decoupled Encoder-Decoder architecture.
The simple architecture achieves competitive results on auto-regressive language modeling and open domain question answering tasks.
The behavior of the proposed model includes grounded context transfer.
The computational implications of retrieval augmented models are discussed in the paper.
Transformers have proven to be powerful language models that capture an impressive amount of world knowledge in its internal parameters.
Transformers generalize to a variety of downstream tasks.
The scaling of model size contributes to the exponential rise of the computation costs.
Model size has a direct impact on the exponential rise of computation costs.
The scaling of model size influences the requirement for a higher number of accelerators.
The model size contributes to the increase in energy consumption.
The scaling of model size affects the increase in the number of parameters.
The external source is typically a massive external database from which information is retrieved.
The retriever embeds the input sequence and retrieves relevant context from an external source.
The retriever component retrieves information from the external source.
The neural network integrates both the input and retrieved external context to predict the target sequence.
The neural network incorporates the retrieved external context into the prediction of target sequence.
Context augmented language model incorporates both the input and retrieved context for prediction.
The context augmented language model integrates both the input and retrieved context for prediction.
Context retrieval and incorporation are key concepts in this context, with a focus on how multiple pieces of context are integrated into a system.
The computationally efficient architecture proposed in the paper is based on a vanilla Encoder-Decoder, emphasizing the separation of context encoding and target sequence prediction.
The model with a simple architecture is shown to be competitive with customized mechanisms such as Chunked-Cross-Attention in language modeling score.
Metrics are defined to measure the utility of the retrieved context and guide the training of the retriever.
Competitive results are shown on downstream tasks of question answering, indicating that the model effectively utilizes the retrieved context.
The model is demonstrated to take advantage of retrieved context without memorizing facts within its internal parameters.
The Encoder-Decoder based architecture proposed in the article incorporates retrieved external context, decoupling context encoding from language model inference.
The proposed model, based on Encoder-Decoder architecture, demonstrates competitive results on auto-regressive language modeling and open domain question answering tasks.
The article analyzes how context improves language modeling prediction on tokens with different linguistic properties and how the model performs grounded context transfer.
The article discusses computational cost and retrieval efficiency in the context of augmentation.
Large language models, often in the form of big neural networks, are trained with a huge amount of training data rich in unstructured knowledge.
Neural networks often store a large amount of memorized information within their weights after model training, which is then leveraged as a knowledge base.
The capability of storing world knowledge is desirable, but it contributes to huge model sizes and the lack of explicit control over the knowledge base.
Memorization contributes to huge model sizes.

At training time, the encoder and decoder are jointly trained.
We first perform ofﬂine retrieval to form triplets of (x, y, c), where c is retrieved by some predeﬁned retriever.

Because the encoding of each context is independent and there is no interaction between context, the attention matrix of encoder is block diagonal and we process them in a linear loop over each diagonal block.
For online language model inference, only the retriever and the decoder are involved.
The retrieval embedding EmbD(c) and the encoder output HEnc(c) of the context are both ofﬂine pre-processed and prepared into the retrieval database of such associated key-value pairs.

When a new input sequence arrives, the retriever is only responsible for the approximate nearest neighbor search and lookup of the associated value that is the pre-computed encoder output. We use mT5 as the backbone architecture for our context augmented Encoder-Decoder.
Train split is used both for training and retrieval.
Validation split is used for bits-per-byte (Bpb) evaluation.
In auto-regressive language model evaluation, due to the fact that text are crawled from web sources, there can be a non-trivial overlap of tokens between the training and validation splits.
In such cases, tokens are often “copy-pasted” from retrieved context into targets without changing.
Such copying leads to near-zero perplexity on targets and has a big effect on final bits-per-byte measurement.
Following the discussion on dataset leakage of Retro, we filtered any example whose targets and context sequences have more than 8 common consecutive tokens.
We found 14.87% of the validation chunks are removed by the filtering of longest common substring.

The goal of context retrieval is to identify the context sequences c∗ that maximize the improvement of some utility function, such as the log-likelihood improvement on target prediction.
Context retrieval aims to maximize the log-likelihood improvement on target prediction, enhancing the utility function of the process.

Noun and number benefited the most from the context

Tokens that are grounded in the retrieved context but did not appear in the input benefit the most from context retrieval.

In this paper, the design of context augmented language model based on Encoder-Decoder architecture is advocated.
The Encoder-Decoder models have the unique computational advantage that the context encoding and language generation are decoupled.
The context encoding can be produced with offline pre-computation and caching.
The decoder is parameter efficient because it is agnostic to the encoder computation.
Context augmentation resulted in big improvements on content words.
The model generates output grounded in retrieved context most of the time due to context augmentation.
Latency improvement from caching is enabled by the decoupled computation.
